refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions scope clarifies the verification boundary and prevents ambiguity in acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end packet loss across the teleoperation communication path (operator input → vehicle → operator feedback) over sustained operational periods. Compare against ≤1% threshold.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator control commands with packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit vehicle telemetry feedback with packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss approaching threshold) to the operator and safety system.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to maintain packet loss below threshold.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) management with packet prioritization, forward error correction (FEC), and dynamic bandwidth allocation.

DP-C: Establish network link quality monitoring with real-time packet loss measurement and operator notification; require customer to provision network infrastructure meeting minimum SLA (≤1% packet loss).
DP-D: Combine DP-B and DP-C: implement QoS management with FEC and prioritization, coupled with continuous link monitoring and customer SLA enforcement.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement adaptive QoS management (packet prioritization, forward error correction, dynamic bandwidth allocation) integrated with continuous network link quality monitoring, packet loss measurement, and operator/safety system alerting; require customer network infrastructure to meet minimum SLA (≤1% packet loss under agreed conditions).

potential_sources: QoS management logic is decoupled from link monitoring logic via a shared packet loss metric interface.
FEC and prioritization are independent algorithms that operate on the same data stream without tight coupling.
Operator alerting and safety system alerting are driven by the same link quality measurement but have independent notification paths (low coupling).
coupling_check.notes: DP-D exhibits acceptable decoupling. The primary integration point is the packet loss measurement and threshold comparison, which is a clean, well-defined interface. No circular dependencies or hidden couplings detected.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer will provision network infrastructure capable of ≤1% packet loss under agreed conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, dedicated VPN, or private network).
ASSUMPTION: Operator and safety system can respond appropriately to packet loss alerts within acceptable time windows.

RISK: If customer network infrastructure is inadequate, QoS and monitoring alone may not achieve ≤1% target; mitigation requires escalation to customer and potential system redesign (e.g., redundant channels).
RISK: FEC adds computational overhead and latency; must validate that FEC latency does not degrade operator control responsiveness.
RISK: Packet loss measurement accuracy depends on telemetry data integrity; circular dependency if telemetry itself is corrupted by packet loss.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify in DP-D the specific FEC algorithm (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes) and QoS prioritization scheme (e.g., operator commands > telemetry > diagnostics). Define packet loss measurement methodology (e.g., sequence number tracking, heartbeat probes) to avoid circular dependency. Establish explicit SLA contract with customer specifying network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth) under which ≤1% packet loss is guaranteed.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication pipeline for remote equipment operation, including QoS management (packet prioritization, FEC), network link quality monitoring, packet loss measurement, and alerting to operator and safety systems. Interfaces with Vehicle for command transmission and telemetry reception, and with Safety Services for safety-critical alerts.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator control commands (e.g., movement, tool actuation) from the tele-operator interface; Teleoperation module encodes, prioritizes, and transmits via Network Link.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state telemetry (position, sensor readings, system status) to the tele-operator display; Teleoperation module receives from Vehicle, applies FEC decoding if needed, measures packet loss, and forwards to operator.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication channel (4G/5G, VPN, or private network) between operator and vehicle; Teleoperation module manages QoS, monitors link quality, and reports packet loss metrics.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Transmits communication degradation alerts (packet loss approaching/exceeding threshold) to Safety Services for operator notification and potential emergency shutdown logic.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning DP-D to NGHE Solution would diffuse QoS and monitoring logic across multiple sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), violating cohesion. Not recommended as primary IM boundary.

Vehicle module is responsible for equipment control and onboard telemetry. QoS management and link monitoring are operator-side concerns, not vehicle-side. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would create tight coupling between vehicle firmware and network management, reducing modularity. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is the natural home for QoS management, link monitoring, and packet loss measurement. All DP-D components (FEC, prioritization, monitoring, alerting) are cohesive within the teleoperation domain. Interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry), Network Link (communication), and Safety Services (SafetyAlert). Highly recommended.

Safety Services owns safety-critical logic and alerting. While SafetyAlert output belongs here, the QoS management and link monitoring logic are not safety-specific; they are communication infrastructure concerns. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would conflate communication quality with safety logic. Not recommended as primary IM.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal IM boundary for DP-D because it owns the end-to-end communication pipeline, has direct access to both Tasking (operator commands) and Telemetry (vehicle feedback) streams, and naturally interfaces with Network Link. QoS management and link monitoring are cohesive communication concerns that belong in a dedicated teleoperation module, not scattered across Vehicle or Safety Services.
containment_risks: RISK: If Teleoperation module becomes too large (QoS + monitoring + alerting + codec management), consider further decomposition into sub-modules (e.g., QoS Manager, Link Monitor, Alert Handler) in a future refinement level.

RISK: Teleoperation module must maintain low coupling with Vehicle module to avoid tight dependencies on vehicle firmware updates. Interface contract (Tasking/Telemetry) must be stable and version-controlled.
RISK: SafetyAlert output to Safety Services must be reliable and low-latency; if Teleoperation module is overloaded, alert propagation may be delayed. Recommend priority queue for safety alerts.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has sufficient computational resources (CPU, memory, network bandwidth) to execute QoS algorithms (FEC, prioritization) and monitoring logic without introducing unacceptable latency. Define clear interface contracts for Tasking (command format, frequency), Telemetry (data format, sampling rate), and SafetyAlert (alert types, priority levels). Establish ownership and maintenance responsibility for Teleoperation module (likely: Tele-operations Team or Network Engineering Team).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (adaptive QoS + link monitoring + alerting) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤1%). The DP provides both proactive measures (FEC, prioritization) to reduce packet loss and reactive measures (monitoring, alerting) to detect and respond to degradation. The DP is implementable and testable against the ≤1% acceptance criterion. No logical gaps or contradictions.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module must not diffuse QoS logic into Vehicle or Safety Services. Maintain clear ownership boundary.
If Teleoperation module grows beyond ~5000 lines of code (estimated), consider sub-module decomposition to avoid monolithic design.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score. All DP-D components (FEC, prioritization, monitoring, alerting) fit naturally within the teleoperation domain. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and stable. No diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible via packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can expose packet loss metrics via telemetry or diagnostic interface for operator/test system to monitor. Acceptance test: measure end-to-end packet loss over 1-hour operational period; confirm ≤1% threshold.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-009 to Teleoperation module is clear. Teleoperation owns the communication pipeline and is responsible for achieving ≤1% packet loss. Vehicle module is responsible for timely command execution and telemetry generation (upstream). Safety Services is responsible for responding to SafetyAlert (downstream). No ambiguity in responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Establish explicit SLA contract with customer specifying network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth, coverage) under which ≤1% packet loss is achievable. Document assumptions in design specification.
Define packet loss measurement methodology in Teleoperation module design (e.g., sequence number tracking, heartbeat probes) to ensure accuracy and avoid circular dependencies.
Specify FEC algorithm and QoS prioritization scheme in DP-D to enable detailed design and implementation.
Plan for sub-module decomposition of Teleoperation if complexity grows (e.g., QoS Manager, Link Monitor, Alert Handler).

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic; it encompasses three distinct sub-requirements (operator command transmission, vehicle telemetry reception, communication degradation detection). DP-D is sound and well-mapped to Teleoperation module. However, to ensure rigorous design and testability, the three sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1, FR-NGHE-009.2, FR-NGHE-009.3) must be decomposed to the next level with explicit DPs and IMs for each. This will clarify ownership, enable independent verification, and reduce risk of missed requirements.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator control commands with packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit vehicle telemetry feedback with packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss approaching threshold) to the operator and safety system.

assumptions: Customer will provision network infrastructure capable of ≤1% packet loss under agreed conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, dedicated VPN, or private network).
Operator and safety system can respond appropriately to packet loss alerts within acceptable time windows.
FEC latency is acceptable and does not degrade operator control responsiveness (to be validated in detailed design).
Packet loss measurement can be performed reliably without circular dependency on corrupted telemetry.
open_risks: If customer network infrastructure is inadequate, QoS and monitoring alone may not achieve ≤1% target; mitigation requires escalation and potential system redesign (e.g., redundant channels).
FEC adds computational overhead; must validate that FEC latency does not exceed operator control loop requirements.
Teleoperation module may grow large if QoS, monitoring, and alerting logic are not carefully modularized; plan for sub-module decomposition.
SafetyAlert propagation latency must be validated to ensure safety-critical response time is met.
evidence_needed: Network SLA contract with customer specifying latency, jitter, bandwidth, and coverage conditions.
Detailed design specification for Teleoperation module including FEC algorithm, QoS prioritization scheme, and packet loss measurement methodology.
Latency analysis for FEC and QoS algorithms to confirm operator control responsiveness is not degraded.
Test plan and acceptance criteria for packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions.
Safety analysis for SafetyAlert propagation latency and operator response time.
